metadata.title: Claude, Not Your Answer.
metadata.thesis: If it does the thinking, you didn't learn — you watched.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Can Claude do your homework for you? Yes. Here is the trade: answers now means worse exam scores later. One study — but the mechanism explains why. Here is what I will do instead: quiz you.

output: here's what I'll do: quiz you instead

beats.narration_text: Here is the data. Bastani et al. 2024 — one study, flag it — found that students who used AI for practice improved on later AI-assisted assessments by around 48 percent. But on a no-AI exam, the same students scored about 17 percent lower than peers who had not leaned on AI. One study — but the mechanism explains why. Practice felt like mastery because it was mastery of AI-assisted practice. The scaffold was still there. The exam removed it.

props.heading: Fluency isn't mastery.
props.body:
Bastani et al. 2024 — single study, flagged.

AI-assisted practice: +48% on AI-assisted assessment.
Same students, no-AI exam: −17% vs control.

The scaffold was still there. The exam removed it.

beats.narration_text: Here is why it works that way. Practice with AI felt like mastery because it was mastery — of AI-assisted practice. The exam removed the scaffold and the skill was not there. That is not a surprise once you understand the mechanism: the struggle is the learning. Retrieval — pulling the answer out yourself — is what wires the memory. Outsource the struggle and you outsource the retention. The tool did the hard part, which was also the useful part.

props.heading: The struggle is the point.
props.body:
Why the gap:
Practice with AI felt like mastery — of AI-assisted practice.
The exam removed the scaffold. The skill wasn't there.

Retrieval = pulling the answer out yourself = wiring the memory.
Outsource the struggle → outsource the retention.

beats.narration_text: The difference is scaffold versus crutch. A crutch does the step for you, never fades, and you cannot do it without the tool. A scaffold holds you while you build strength, is meant to fade, and then you take it from there. Same tool. Different use. A scaffold is what you want — you are the one who ends up strong.

props.heading: Scaffold, not crutch.
props.body:
Crutch:
— does the step for you
— doesn't fade
— you can't do it without the tool

Scaffold:
— holds you while you build strength
— meant to fade
— you take it from there

beats.narration_text: Commit before the reveal. Which of these four uses is a scaffold, not a crutch? A: have Claude answer the question. B: have Claude quiz you. C: have Claude write your essay. D: have Claude read your essay and give feedback. Pick one before the next beat flips.

props.command: which use is a scaffold? A) answer the question B) quiz you C) write your essay D) give feedback on your essay
props.runningText: commit to A, B, C, or D…

beats.narration_text: B and D are scaffolds. Quiz you — you are doing the retrieval, Claude is the stimulus. Feedback on your draft — you wrote it, Claude is reading it. A and C are crutches: answer the question, write the essay. You are watching. The pair rule is how you hold the line: every AI use that does the work for you has a version where you do the work and AI supports it. Use the second version.

props.heading: The pair rule.
props.body:
Scaffold uses (B, D):
— quiz me on this topic  ✓
— give feedback on my draft  ✓

Crutch uses (A, C):
— answer it for me  ✗
— write the draft  ✗

Every 'do it for me' has a 'help me do it' version. Use that one.

beats.narration_text: The trade in full. Answers now costs you exam points later — Bastani 2024, one study, the mechanism holds. The struggle is the retention mechanism; outsource it and you outsource the learning. Scaffold beats crutch: you write the draft, Claude reads it. You retrieve the answer, Claude quizzes you. The pair rule keeps you on the right side of both the learning and the integrity line.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, Not Your Answer — one card
artifactLines: The data: AI-assisted practice gains (+48%) collapse on no-AI exams (−17%) — Bastani 2024, single study, mechanism confirmed.
The mechanism: the struggle IS the retention mechanism. Outsource the retrieval and you outsource the memory.
Scaffold not crutch: you write the draft, Claude reads it. You retrieve, Claude quizzes you. You do the hard part.
The pair rule: every 'Claude, do it for me' has a 'Claude, help me do it' version. Use that one.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude with whatever topic you are studying. It will quiz you — not give you answers. That is the scaffold. After three questions you will know exactly where your gaps are.

props.command: Quiz me on [topic] — don't give me the answers. Ask one question at a time, wait for my response, then tell me if I'm right and why. After 3 questions, show me where my gaps are.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Not Your Answer. If the trade makes sense, subscribe. HAI.
